coredata.dc:title: Novel 0D-1D-2D nanostructured MCN/NCDs recyclable composite for boosted peroxymonosulfate activation under visible light toward tetracycline degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Xueding
Xiao, Kaibang
Liu, Zhang
Xu, Weicheng
Liang, Fawen
Mo, Shengpeng
Wu, Xiaolian
Beiyuan, Jingzi
coredata.dc:description:
In this study, a novel nitrogen modified carbon quantum dots (NCDs) decorated graphite carbon nitride /molybdenum (Mo) heterojunction photocatalyst (MCN/NCDs) was obtained by in-situ calcination and physical deposition, and applied as peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activators for tetracycline (TC) removal under visible light. It was found that the MCN/NCDs as PMS activation catalyst exhibited outstanding removal efficiency under visible light assistance and nearly 98.4% of TC was degraded within 60 min. The analysis data of photochemistry demonstrated that the introduction of NCDs could significantly improve the visible-light utilization, narrow the band gap and increase the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The results of radical capture experiment and electron spin resonance test (ESR) suggested that the order of active species contributing to TC degradation in vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system was O2
– > h+>
OH > 1O2 > SO4
−. Moreover, the MCN/NCDs composite exhibited excellent stability with over 98% activity retention for 5 cycles and superior catalytic efficiency in four real water matrix. Finally, the underlying degradation mechanism and toxicity assessment were proposed in detail. This work provided a new perspective on the design of excellent activity g-C3N4 composite catalysts and elucidated a promising potential strategy for efficient contaminated water treatment via PMS activation process.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel 0D-1D-2D nanostructured MCN/NCDs recyclable composite was synthesized as an effective photo-assisted activator of PMS with high-efficiency for organic contaminants.

In this study, a novel nitrogen modified carbon quantum dots (NCDs) decorated graphite carbon nitride /molybdenum (Mo) heterojunction photocatalyst (MCN/NCDs) was obtained by in-situ calcination and physical deposition, and applied as peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activators for tetracycline (TC) removal under visible light. It was found that the MCN/NCDs as PMS activation catalyst exhibited outstanding removal efficiency under visible light assistance and nearly 98.4% of TC was degraded within 60 min. The analysis data of photochemistry demonstrated that the introduction of NCDs could significantly improve the visible-light utilization, narrow the band gap and increase the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The results of radical capture experiment and electron spin resonance test (ESR) suggested that the order of active species contributing to TC degradation in vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system was O2
– > h+>
OH > 1O2 > SO4
−. Moreover, the MCN/NCDs composite exhibited excellent stability with over 98% activity retention for 5 cycles and superior catalytic efficiency in four real water matrix. Finally, the underlying degradation mechanism and toxicity assessment were proposed in detail. This work provided a new perspective on the design of excellent activity g-C3N4 composite catalysts and elucidated a promising potential strategy for efficient contaminated water treatment via PMS activation process.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC), as a universal antibiotic, is commonly used in medical treatment and animal husbandry because of its unique function as an antibacterial agent, which can be frequently detected in surface water and even underwater (ranging 5.4–8.1 ng·L–1) [1,2]. However, TC can cause serious risks to the ecosystem and human health owing to its solubility in water, strong bactericidal properties, apparent ecotoxicity and potential carcinogenicity [3]. Therefore, it is urgent to devise an efficient, low-cost, and environmentally friendly technology for the removal of TC from wastewater.

In recent years, semiconductor photocatalytic technology has been proved as an efficient advanced oxidation method for wastewater remediation [4,5]. For semiconductor materials, visible light responsive graphite carbon nitride (CN) photocatalyst has drawn extensive attention to perform selective catalytic oxidation due to its unique physicochemical and thermodynamic properties [6,7]. However, ordinary CN had low efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs separation, and the relatively low specific surface area that might restrict its practical application [8,9]. To solve the above problems, various modification strategies have been developed [10], such as element doping [11,12], heterostructure constructing [13,14] and morphology controlling [15,16]. Among them, constructing heterostructure has been proved to be an effective strategy to enhance the photocatalytic performance of CN. Some studies have reported that transition metal molybdenum doped CN showed excellent performance for organic pollutants removal [17]. Xue et al. [18] fabricated MoO3/g-C3N4 nanoparticles through a simple mixing and annealing method, which showed a higher RhB degradation rate than that of bulk CN. Yan et al. [19] fabricated Mo/g-C3N4 and found that the charge separation of g-C3N4 was enhanced through interlayer pathway after the intercalation of MoO3-x. Moreover, to further achieve excellent photocatalytic performance, different dimensional structures, 0D, 1D, and 2D materials, have been employed to design the multidimensional photocatalyst [20,21]. The construction of multi-dimensional nanostructures is beneficial to the fast charge transfer across interfaces, and accelerates the catalytic activity. Wu and co-workers have fabricated hierarchical 0D/1D/2D nanohybrids Ag/Nb2O5@Nb2CTx, and found that the intimate contact between Nb2CTx sheets and Nb2O5 nanorods facilitated the fast transfer of photogenerated carriers [22]. N-doped carbon dot (NCDs), as a novel zero-dimensional carbon material less than 10 nm in size, are gaining interest due to the distinct advantages of low toxicity, the excellent electron donors and electron acceptors and up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) property [23–25]. Wang et al. [26] reported that cooperating NCDs with CN effectively enhanced the separation of electron-hole pair, and promoted indomethacin (IDM) degradation and reduced the toxicity of IDM solutions. Jia et al. [27] found that doping NCDs in g-C3N4/WO3 heterojunctions significantly improve visible light utilization and promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. However, the inclusions of Mo metal and NCDs into g-C3N4 for enhancing the activity were seldomly reported.

In addition, coupling photocatalysis with other advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) was also an efficient strategy to further improve the removal efficiency [28]. Oxidants as important factors in AOPs, including H2O2 [29], peroxymonosulfate (PMS) [30] and peroxydisulfate (PDS) [31], have been investigated as promotors for the tremendous enhancement of photocatalytic performance. Among the above oxidants, PMS is widely used for organic pollutants removal in water environment due to its low cost, non-toxicity, asymmetric structure, and the ease of transport and storage [32,33]. Therefore, this study reported a simplistic method to fabricate the Mo and NCDs co-doped g-C3N4 (MCN/NCDs) composite, and evaluated their PMS activation performance for TC removal under visible light irradiation. The underlying mechanism and toxicity assessment for the degradation of TC in vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system were also presented. Four main aspects can be concluded in this work: (i) photo-assisted PMS activation performance by MCN/NCDs; (ii) the stability and practical applications by vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system; (iii) the mechanism of photo-assisted PMS activation by MCN/NCDs; (iv) the degradation pathways and toxicity assessment analysis for TC degradation by the vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs process.

Melamine (C3H6N6), molybdenyl acetylacetonate (C10H14MoO6), potassium peroxymonosulfate (PMS) triple salt (2KHSO5·KHSO4·K2SO4), and citric acid (C6H8O7) were provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. The chemicals, including NaCl, Na2SO4, NaHCO3 and CH4N2O were bought from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Tetracycline (C22H24N2O8), Methyl Orange (C14H14N3SO3Na), Methylene Blue (C16H18ClN3S), p-benzoquinone (C6H4O2) and L-histidine (C6H9N3O2) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O), tert-butanol (C4H10O) and ethanol (C2H5OH) were obtained from Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd. All the other chemicals were at least of analytic grade and used without further purification.

The preparation of MCN/NCDs includes four steps. (i) The g-C3N4 sample was achieved by a simple calcining method. A certain amount of melamine was calcined at 550 °C for 4 h with 10 °C·min−1 heating rate under air atmosphere. The obtained yellow g-C3N4 was collected and named as CN. (ii) Preparation of MCN sample. The melamine (12.6 g) was dispersed into deionized water (60 mL) with a certain amount of molybdenyl acetylacetonate. Then, stirred magnetically at 60 °C for 1 h and dried at 60 °C. The obtained catalyst was further calcined at the same process as (i). The obtained powder was collected and named as MCN. (iii) Preparation of NCDs. A certain amount of urea and 9.0 g of citric acid were dissolved into deionized water (45 mL), then placed on an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After hydrothermal process, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min, and the as-prepared NCDs solution was placed at 4 °C for subsequent synthetic procedures. (iv) The MCN/NCDs samples were obtained by a solvent evaporation method. 0.5 g of MCN powder and a certain amount of NCDs were dissolved into 60 mL anhydrous ethanol with ultrasonic 1 h. Then, vigorous stirred for 10 h until the complete solvent evaporation. The dried samples were ground and further heated under N2 flow at 300 °C for 1 h, and named as MCN/NCDs in this study.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of MCN/NCDs were observed by Rigoku SmartLab with Cu Kα radiation. The surface morphology of the composites was measured by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Quanta 400 FEG, FEI, America) equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) and the surface distribution was recorded using highresolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, America). N2 adsorption instrument were used to measure the pore size distribution and specific area of the composites via Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method (TriStar II 3020, Micromeritics). Nicolet Nexus 470 spectrometer with KBr as a reference was applied to collect the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) information. The Raman spectra were obtained on laser Raman spectrometer (Renishaw RM2000, Renishaw, England) that operated with 514 nm laser excitation. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) pattern of as-prepared catalysts was determined by a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer equipped with an Al Kα X-ray source. The UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) were obtained by a Lambda 650 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra combined with a Hitachi F-4600 spectrophotometer were performed on an excitation wavelength of 375 nm. The transient photocurrent measurements and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were conducted on an electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, China). Total organic carbon (TOC) levels were measured using a TOC analyzer (Multi N/ C 2100, Analytikjena, Germany).

An 800 W xenon lamp was used as the visible light source equipped with a cutoff filter (k > 420 nm). Typically, all the TC degradation tests were carried out by mixing 50 mg of photocatalysts and 10 mg/L of TC (100 mL). The adsorption–desorption equilibrium of the above solution was determined to be 30 min in the dark. After that, PMS was added into the above mixture and concurrently start the catalytic processes under visible light irradiation. At every 10 min intervals, 2 mL of above aqueous was withdrawn and filtered, and the concentration was detected by UV–vis spectra instrument at the maximum absorption wavelength (TC is 357 nm). The degradation percentage (η%) can be calculated as follows:
(1)
η %
=
(
C 0
-
C t
)
/
C 0
× 100 %
where η% is the degradation rate of TC; C0 and Ct represent the initial and t time concentration of the pollution, respectively.
The catalyst was washed with ethanol and distilled water three times after each use. Finally, the catalyst was dried in an oven at 60 °C for subsequent recycle experiments.

According to the SEM results, pure g-C3N4 sample (Fig. S1a) appear to be a lamellar structure with relatively smooth monolayer surface, and still maintain this morphology after the introduction of molybdenum (Fig. S1b). TEM image (Fig. 1 a and Fig. 1b) further verifies the nanosheets-assembled hierarchical structure of MCN/NCDs. It can be observed that MoO3 is 1D belts structure with the width from 10 to 50 nm uniform anchoring on the g-C3N4 sheet, which can enhance the intimate interface contact and benefit on the formation of the Z-scheme passageway. Moreover, some uniformly disperse black dots with particle size of about 1–3 nm are observed on MCN/NCDs sample, suggesting that NCDs are successfully located on the interface of MCN. NCDs, acting as active site and collector, can effectively promote charge carriers separation and enhance photocatalytic performance [23]. The 0D/1D/2D hierarchical structure can effectively separate the gaps between g-C3N4 layers, thereby increasing the accessibility of reactants to active sites of the MCN/NCDs, and promote catalytic performance. [22,34]. As displayed in Fig. 1b, the interlayer spacing of 0.131 nm is found and it is attributed to the (271) plane of MoO3 (JCPDS No. 35-0609). EDS results (Fig. 1 c and d) depict that C, N, O and Mo elements are favorably dispersed within the MCN/NCDs.

The prepared samples exhibit typical IV-type isotherms (Fig. S2), suggesting the existence of mesoporous characteristic of the catalysts. The surface area, pore diameter and pore volume of the obtained samples are also showed in Table S1. Compared to pure g-C3N4, both MCN and MCN/NCDs catalysts exhibit high surface area. Two factors may be concluded: one is the interaction between the two semiconductors (g-C3N4 and MoO3) [35], and the other is attributed to the release of gases during the decomposition of molybdenyl acetylacetonate and carbon quantum dots modified by urea [36]. Large surface area and pore volume may provide more active sites to further activate PMS, and at last promote TC degradation [37].

Fig. 2 illustrates the XRD results of raw CN, MCN and MCN/NCDs composite. The image shows that two peaks centered at around 13.0° and 27.4° are ascribed to the in-plane structural packing motif of tri-s-triazine units in g-C3N4 and the (002) crystal planes of g-C3N4 relating to interlayer-stacking structure of conjugated aromatic system, respectively [38]. Interestingly, these two peaks become broader and weaker with the addition of NCDs in MCN, indicating that the introduction of NCDs in carbon nitride matrix contribute to the structural distortion of the polymer [39]. A small new peak at 23.3° is found in MCN sample that ascribed to the MoO3 particles [18], while disappears after NCDs modification. The crystallite sizes of CN in MCN and MCN/NCDs are respectively calculated as approximately 6.92 nm and 6.47 nm using Debye-Scherrer equation [40]. The reduction in crystallite sizes implies the amorphous transformation of the catalyst, which provides a strong band tail to enhance the light absorption [41]. Moreover, the as-prepared catalysts display similar FT-IR spectra (Fig. S3), and peaks centred at 807 cm−1 and 1237–1652 cm−1 are attributed to the breathing mode of the triazine units and the stretching mode of CN heterocycles, respectively [27,42]. These results demonstrate that the chemical framework of carbon nitride keep well after being modified by Mo and NCDs. The Raman spectra of CN, MCN and MCN/NCDs are shown in Fig. S4. Typical D (1350 cm−1) and G bands (1580 cm−1) are observed for all the samples. Generally, the relative intensity of the D and G peaks (ID/IG) of a carbon material suggest its defect degree [43]. Compared with pristine CN, doping Mo and NCDs can improve the defect degree of CN.

XPS analysis of MCN/NCDs sample is conducted in detail. Fig. S5 demonstrate that both MCN and MCN/NCDs nanocomposites consist of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O) and molybdenum (Mo) elements and the atomic percentages of elements in MCN/NCDs are shown in Table S2 and S3. As seen in Fig. 3 a, two peaks located at 284.6 eV and 288.0 eV in the C 1 s spectra are the results of sp2 C-C bonds and sp2 hybridized NCN, respectively [44,45]. For N 1 s spectrum in MCN sample (Fig. 3b), pyridinic N pyrrolic N and graphitic N are found and locate at 398.5, 399.7 and 400.7 eV, respectively [46,47]. For O 1 s spectrum of the samples (Fig. 3c), 530.4, 531.7 and 532.5 eV are attributed to the MoO/CNO, OH bonds and O adsorbed H2O, respectively [48–50]. MCN/NCDs complex shows lower binding energy as compared to MCN, suggesting the strong interaction between the NCDs and MCN. The Mo 3d peak is further examined and the results are displayed in Fig. 3d. In MCN composites, the fitted Mo 3d spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks, that centering at 232.2 and 235.6 eV are correspond to Mo 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 of Mo6+ peaks and the other at 251.1 eV is assigned to the oxidation state of Mo5+ [18,51]. Interestingly, a new characteristic peak (231.2 eV) is found after the introduction of NCDs onto the MCN, which can be ascribed to MoC bond and Mo5+ [19,52].

The PL spectra is used to analyze the optical characteristics of catalysts. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the PL emission intensity of MCN is lower than CN, which can be attributed to a decline in the charge carrier density or a resistance of the charge carrier’s recombination. Noteworthy, the PL intensity of the MCN/NCDs composite is comparatively lower than MCN, which may facilitate to the photodegradation activity. Moreover, the ability of light absorption and charge migration behaviors are shown in Fig. 4b, the edges of the absorption peaks of CN (463 nm), MCN (473 nm), and MCN/NCDs (484 nm) correspond to their band gap energies of 2.68 and 2.62 and 2.56 eV, respectively [53]. The MCN/NCDs composite exhibits a narrower band gap than other samples, which may be due to the construction of CN/MoO3 heterojunction and the up-conversion photoluminescence characteristics of NCDs [27,54]. The band structures of MCN as well as MCN/NCDs are further characterized by valence band XPS (VB-XPS). As displayed in Fig. 4c, the VB maxima of MCN and MCN/NCDs are at 2.18 eV and 2.13 eV, respectively. The band gap potentials of the CB and VB potentials of MoO3 and CN in MCN/NCDs are tested by UV–vis DRS and XPS-VB and the results are shown in Fig. S6 and Fig. 4d. The generated electrons on the CB of MoO3 quickly migrated to the VB of CN, constructing a Z-scheme charge transfer route. The charge separation is thus effectively enhanced, and remain electrons and holes with stronger redox capabilities on the CB of g-C3N4 and VB of MoO3, respectively. In addition, doping with NCDs can further enhance the absorption of visible light and effectively improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and it may also provide more electrons for PMS activation and facilitate the generation of reactive oxygen species.

To further determine the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers in different catalysts, the photoelectrochemical properties of the MCN/NCDs, MCN and CN are studied using transient photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As shown in Fig. 5 a, it can be seen that the MCN/NCDs photocatalyst displays stronger photocurrent intensity compared to MCN and CN under visible light irradiation, implying that the introduction of Mo and NCDs facilitates the charge migration and separation which induces superior photocatalysis [55]. The arc radius on the EIS Nyquist plot of MCN/NCDs is much smaller than that of MCN and CN (Fig. 5b), indicating that the lower impedance and stronger electron transfer ability due to the loading of MoO3 and NCDs on the g-C3N4 matrix [56]. Above EIS results are in harmony with the PL results as well.

The adsorption performances of TC over different materials are displayed in Fig.S7. It is found that pure CN has almost no adsorption for TC. After the doping of metal Mo, the adsorption performance of MCN is significantly improved, about 13% of TC is adsorbed within 30 min. In comparison with CN and MCN, the co-doping sample (MCN/NCDs) shows much higher adsorption capacity toward TC (22.1%). As shown in Fig. S8, there is a negligible change in TC concentration within 60 min under visible-light irradiation alone, while almost no change in TC removal under PMS alone, indicating TC can be hardly removed under the visible light irradiation or PMS alone. It is found only 52.1%, 56.3% and 58.9% of TC are removed in PMS/CN, PMS/MCN and PMS/MCN/NCDs system, respectively (Fig. S8a). Visible light irradiation is benefit for TC degradation in catalysts/PMS system, implying the active species are produced in this process and contributes to the TC removal. For the CN sample, the degradation rate of TC is about 8.7% within 60 min reaction. Compared with it, the TC removal increases to 60.1% and 78.9% for MCN and MCN/NCDs in the same duration, respectively (Fig. S8b). It confirms the pollutant removal by MCN/NCDs can be significantly improved under photo-assistance, which is ascribed to: (i) co-doping of Mo and NCDs significantly improve the visible-light utilization; (ii) NCDs serve as the center for photogenerated electrons gathering, thus accelerating the carriers separation; (iii) the enhanced surface area (by Mo and NCDs) increases reaction active sites (0D-1D-2D nanostructure).

Interestingly, combining PMS and visible light in catalyst system, the removal efficiency for TC is significantly improved. In vis/PMS/catalyst processes (Fig. 6 ), about 75.6% and 83.8% of TC are eliminated within 60 min for CN and MCN, respectively. Up to 98 % TC is removed in the vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system within 60 min, demonstrating that NCDs modification greatly improves the PMS catalytic activity of MCN under photo-assistance. Besides, the mineralization of TC is also enhanced by the doping of NCDs (Fig. S9). It is found that vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs exhibits excellent performance for TC removal compare with other vis/PMS/catalysts system as listed in Table S4.

As shown in Fig. 6b, the degradation process of TC in vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system can be well fitted with the pseudo-first-order kinetic model. The k value for TC degradation is 0.0649 min−1 in vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system, which is 2.7 and 2.2 time faster than that of CN (k = 0.0242 min−1) and MCN (k = 0.0301 min−1) systems, respectively. The enhanced performance can be ascribed to the coupling of nanomaterials of different dimensions that promotes the efficient charge migration through the synergistic effect, and at last prolongs the lifetime of the charge carriers. Besides, a construction of Z-scheme heterojunction g-C3N4-MoO3 and the presence of NCDs as the significant contributor will contribute to the provision of more electrons for PMS activation, and accelerates pollutants degradation in water [57].

To further determine the activity of vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system in complex media, different factors are carried out. As show in Fig. 7 a, a positive correlation between catalyst concentration and degradation efficiency is observed when the MCN/NCDs concentration increases from 0.25 g/L to 0.50 g/L. However, a slight inhibition of removal efficiency occurs as further increases to 0.75 g/L, which can be attributed to the deficiency of photons required for the excitation of electrons in MCN/NCDs [58] or the light scattering/attenuation effect in TC solution by the overdose of catalyst [59]. Moreover, the apparent degradation rate constant increases from 0.0255 to 0.0649 min−1 (Fig. 7b) when the dosage of PMS enlarges from 0.25 to 0.50 mM, while further increase of PMS dosage does not induce an obvious improvement or even inhibited the degradation efficiency. The inhibition effect can be attributed to the scavenging of radicals by superabundant oxidants and/or the self-combination reactions of radicals (Eqs. (2)–(3)) [40,60].

As depicted in Fig. 7c, the removal rate decreases from 98.4% to 85.0% when TC concentration increases from 0.010 g/L to 0.025 g/L. Two possible reasons can be concluded: (i) high TC concentration inevitably generate more intermediates, which will consume some active radicals. (ii) the transmittance of solution decreases, and affects the light utilization efficiency of the catalyst [59]. The influence of pH on TC removal in vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system are also carried out by setting initial solution pH from 2.98 to 10.04. As displayed in Fig. 7d, the kinetic constant of reaction gradually enhances as the pH values increase to 4.69, while only a slightly decline in TC removal in strong alkaline condition, indicating the excellent pH adaptability of vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system. The inhibited TC removal at pH = 2.98 is likely because PMS is more stable and may hardly to be activated under acidic conditions [61]. The zero point of charge (pHzpc) of MCN/NCDs is determined to be 6.7 as shown from the results in Fig. S10. Hence, the MCN/NCDs is positively charged at acidic pH and negatively charged at alkaline pH. The electrostatic the repulsion between the TC and catalyst surface charges at alkaline pH creates a barrier and inhibits the degradation. In addition, high concentrations of OH– will consume SO4·-and produce ·OH with a lower redox potential [62]. The effects of several kinds of anions such as Cl–, SO4 2–, HPO4 2– and HCO3 – are also examined. As shown in Fig. S11, Cl– and SO4 2– show slight inhibition effects on TC removal within 60 min, possibly due to the elimination of the high oxidation potential of active radicals (
OH and SO4
−) (Eqs. (4)–(7)) [63,64]. HPO4 2– and HCO3 – display a slightly positive effect on the TC degradation, which can be attributed to the directly PMS activation that form more active species [65–67].
(2) SO4
− + HSO5 – → SO5
– + HSO4 –
(3) SO4
− + SO4
− → S2O8 2–
(4) Cl– + SO4
− → SO4 2– + Cl
(5) Cl– +
OH → ClHO–
(6) SO4 2– + SO4
− → S2O8 2– + e–
(7) SO4 2– + ·OH → SO4
− + OH–

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential of the developed vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the stability of MCN/NCDs, the recycling test is performed for five consecutive reaction cycles (Fig. 8 ). It is interesting to note that an enhancement in TC degradation is presented with the increase of cycles from 1st run to 3rd run, and the degradation rate constants also increase slightly from 0.0598 min−1 to 0.0846 min−1. After five successive runs, the MCN/NCDs catalyst still maintained superior catalytic performance than the initial use. As can be observed in Table S5, the atomic percentage of O of the used MCN/NCDs sample increases from 3.16% to 3.69% after reaction. Significantly, the O 1 s spectra shows the increase of OH from 22.9 % to 40.4 % (Fig. S12). The enhanced performance of the spent catalyst may be ascribed to the repeated wetting of MCN/NCDs after each reuse, in which the hydroxyl groups formed on the catalyst surface. The oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of catalyst can promote the activation of PMS [68]. Moreover, photo-activation of the catalyst after light exposure may also contribute to the performance of the reused catalyst [58]. The relevant results can be found in several cited papers [58]. The excellent stability may due to the coupling of nanomaterials of different dimensions (0D-1D-2D) that promotes the efficient charge migration through the synergistic effect. Moreover, the stability of MCN is also carried out and the result is showed in Fig. S13. TC removal efficiency decreased from 83.5% of the first cycle to 67.9% of the fifth cycles in vis/PMS/MCN system, indicating that NCDs plays a crucial role in improving the stability of MCN/NCDs.

Analysis of the crystal structure and chemical properties of the used MCN/NCDs using XRD and XPS characterisation techniques. As shown in Fig. S14, the crystalline structure and chemical elements of the recycled MCN/NCDs remains almost the unchanged as the fresh catalyst. Significantly, the Mo 3d spectra shows the increase of Mo6+ from 61.8 % to 65.4 % and decrease of Mo5+ from 38.2% to 34.6% (Fig. S12), suggesting that Mo5+ may be involved in the activation of PMS for the degradation of TC. The concentrations of Mo ions in the vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system are studied by ICP-MS (PerkinElmer Avio200). The results show that the leaching of Mo ions for MCN/NCDs is significantly reduced compared with the catalyst of MCN without NCDs doping (Fig. S15).

To explore the feasibility of the prepared MCN/NCDs catalyst in eliminating TC from real aquatic systems, the degradation reaction was carried out in different real water systems, including tap water, artificial seawater and wastewater treatment plant (WTP) effluent (from an urban sewage treatment plant in Foshan City, Guangdong Province). The artificial seawater is composed of 26.5 g/L NaCl, 24 g/L MgCl2, 0.73 g/L KCl, 3.3 g/L MgSO4, 0.2 g/L NaHCO3, 1.1 g/L CaCl2 and 0.28 g/L NaBr [69]. As depicted in Fig. 8c, over 90% of TC is removed in these four water matrix, indicating that the aqueous bases have no significant effect on pollutant removal and the vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system is more stable than the other catalytic systems [70,71]. A slightly decline in TC removal efficiency WTP effluent system is expectable due to the high concentrations of organics, which may scavenge the reactive radical species [72]. Moreover, different dyes pollutants (including Methyl orange, Rhodamine B, and Methylene blue) are also carried out to evaluate the removal efficiency and selectivity in the vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs process. Interestingly, all these three dyes can be also completely degraded within 60 min in vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system (Fig. 8d). Therefore, it can be concluded MCN/NCDs has great potential for practical applications in vis/PMS/catalyst processes.

To identify the main active radicals in vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system, the quenching tests are carried out with the addition of radical scavengers, including ethanol (EtOH, 100 mM, scavenger of SO4
−/
OH), tert-butanol (TBA, 100 mM, scavenger of
OH), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ, 1 mM, scavenger of O2
–), L-histidine (1 mM, scavenger of 1O2) and triethanolamine (ETOA, 1 mM, scavenger of h+) [64,73,74]. As showed in Fig. 9 a and Fig. S16, the degradation rate of TC decreases after adding a certain amount of EtOH or TBA into solution, and the inhibitory effect of EtOH (inhibition of 38.3%) is greater than that of TBA (inhibition of 25.8%), indicating that both SO4
− and
OH participate in TC degradation. Moreover, the removal rate of TC is obviously reduced from 98.4% to 64.8% (p-BQ), 80.7% (L-histidine) and 67.1% (ETOA) in vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs, respectively, demonstrating that O2
–, 1O2 and h+ may be generated and play pivotal roles in TC degradation. Furthermore, the contribution on TC removal rates by radical and non-radical reactive species can be roughly estimated based on the kinetic data (Eqs. (S5) and (S6)) [75], and it can be achieved that the main effect of free radical pathway is up to 75.3% in TC degradation process. According to the quenching experiment results, the influence of active species is shown in the follow order: O2
– > h+ >
OH > 1O2 > SO4
−.

To further make clear the roles of different active species in the degradation processes, the ESR spectra with 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidine (TEMP) or 5-dimethyl-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as spin trapping agents are applied to check the ROS generation and consumption process. As shown in Fig. 9b, the intensity of DMPO-
OH and DMPO-SO4
− signals form in vis/MCN/NCDs system are slightly enhanced after the introduction of PMS, indicating that more
OH and SO4
− are formed in vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system. The same phenomenon is found in DMPO-O2
– result, where the strength of DMPO-O2
– adduct in vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system is much higher than that in vis/MCN/NCDs (Fig. 9c). As for 1O2, the triplet signals with equal intensities are also found in vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system (Fig. 9d). These phenomena are in accordance with the radical quenching experiment that O2
–,
OH, 1O2 and SO4
− are formed in vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system and are participated in photocatalytic activation of PMS toward TC removal.

A possible catalytic mechanism is proposed as exhibited in Fig. 10 . The electrons in MCN/NCDs can be excited to its conduction band (CB) under visible light irradiation, while the holes remain on its valence band (Eq. (8)). The photogenerated electrons can react with HSO5 – to produce SO4
− and
OH, and also promote the reduction of the adsorbed O2 to O2
– by Equations (9) and (10) [6]. The formed O2
– can be further oxidized by photogenerated holes to produce 1O2 through Eq. (11) [76]. Meanwhile, the reaction between photogenerated holes and PMS (Eqs. (12)–(13) may also induce the formation of 1O2 [77]. Interestingly, introducing NCDs into the MCN photocatalyst system can greatly promote the effective transfer of photogenerated electrons and enhance the visible light, and at last form more reactive oxygen species. Besides, molybdenum ion of low chemical valence state (Mo5+) can activate PMS through electron transfer to generate active radical SO4
− (Eq. (14)) [78]. NCDs loaded on MCN may efficiently reduce the recombination of photoexcited charge carriers and improve the charge transfer efficiency. The action of conduction electrons and holes in Mo-containing photocatalyst can accelerate the cycle of Mo bonds with different valence states, and improve the stability of the catalyst (Eq. (15)) [27,60]. Therefore, radical and nonradical reactive species may contribute to TC molecules destruction, and the pollutant will be degraded into small compounds or even mineralized into CO2, H2O, etc (Eq. (16)).
(8) MCN/NCDs + hv → e– + h+
(9) HSO5 – + e– → SO4
− +
OH
(10) O2 + e– → O2
–
(11) O2
– + h+ → 1O2
(12) HSO5 – + h+ → SO5
– + H+
(13) 2SO5
– + H2O → HSO4 – + 1.51O2
(14) Mo5+ + HSO5 – → Mo6+ + SO4
− + OH−
(15) Mo6+ + e– → Mo5+
(16) O2
–, h+,
OH, 1O2 or SO4
− + TC → … → small molecules

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed degradation pathway of TC by the vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system
ce:section.ce:para: Although 98.4% of TC is removed in vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system within 60 min, the TOC removal efficiency was only 15.7%, most of pollutant is degraded into intermediates during the catalytic process. Eleven intermediate products are detected during TC degradation in the vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system by HPLC-MS (Fig. S17 and Table S6). To investigate the mechanism in depth, Fukui functions are utilized to predict the active sites for ROS attack. It is generally believed that the larger ƒ0 of the reaction sites are, the greater reactivity can be achieved [74]. As shown in Fig. S18 and Table S7, the reactive sites of TC that easily to be attacked by reactive species are marked, including N 42 (ƒ0 = 0.127), O 28 (ƒ0 = 0.054), O 25 (ƒ0 = 0.051), C 5 (ƒ0 = 0.041), C 12 (ƒ0 = 0.040) and C 47(ƒ0 = 0.034). Based on the above results, three possible pathways are proposed (Fig. 11 ). The degradation processes of TC including demethylation, deamidation, deamination, hydroxylation, and ring-opening reactions are occurred in vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system [79]. In pathway I, the N42 atom with the highest ƒ0 (0.0.1270) is easy to be attacked by active radicals, leading the formation of P1. And compound P1 undergoes amine groups elimination and deamidation, CC bonds breakage, and α-position of carbonyl removal leading to the formation of P2 and P3, which are in good agreement with the previous reports [80,81]. Pathway Ⅱ is that TC is dissociated into P4 (m/z = 61) and P5 (m/z = 388) with the attack of C5 atom (ƒ0 = 0.041) via removing of CONH2 and OH. Then P5 undergo open-ring reactions and the dissociation of some functional groups to form a series of small by-procucts, such as P6 (m/z = 276), P7 (m/z = 148) and P8 (m/z = 74) [80,82]. In the pathway III, the subsequent demethylation reaction of TC lead to the formation of P9 (m/z = 399), which is further oxidized into P10 (m/z = 315) and P11 (m/z = 191) through ring dehiscence [83]. Finally, the intermediate compounds are oxidized to small molecular products, CO2 and H2O.

The Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.) program is applied to evaluate the acute toxicity LD50, developmental toxicity, mutagenicity and bioaccumulation factor of TC and its intermediates [38,84]. The LD50 of TC for rats is 1401 mg/kg, which is considered as “toxic”. As shown in Fig. 12 a, most of intermediates have lower LD50 values than TC except the detected ring-opening intermediates (i.e., P4, P7 and P8), indicating that the acute toxicity is reduced after degradation in vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system for 60 min. In addition, the developmental toxicity of most intermediate byproducts for TC removal in vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs is reduced, whereas the P6 and P7 became “developmental non-toxic” (Fig. 12b). It is worth noting that the bioaccumulative factors of most of TC intermediates increase significantly (Fig. 12c). The mutagenicity of TC and degradation intermediates is shown in Fig. 12d. Particularly, TC molecule is mutagenicity positive and most of the TC intermediates show lower mutagenicity values. The detected final intermediate of P8 is mutagenicity negative and its mutagenicity values is also lower than 0.2, which is largely reduced compare to TC (0.56).

In this paper, a novel 0D-1D-2D nanostructured MCN/NCDs composite was successfully fabricated and combined with visible light and PMS toward the removal of TC pollutant. A construction of Z-scheme heterojunction g-C3N4-MoO3 and the presence of NCDs as the significant contributor can enhance the charge carrier separation, and contribute to the provision of more electrons for the activation of PMS to generate free radicals, leading to the significantly improvement of photo-assisted activation of PMS performance for TC degradation. Specifically, vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system also shows excellent TC removal efficiency in different real aquatic environment, over 90% TC is removed in each of the four various water matrix. Inorganic anionic tests also indicate that HPO4 2– and HCO3 – can improve the degradation performance, while Cl– and SO4 2– exhibit slight negative effect toward TC removal. The mechanism of catalytic reaction for photo-assisted activation of PMS is illustrated and the influences of active species are shown in follow order: O2
– > h+>
OH > 1O2 > SO4
−. The recycling test and toxicity assessment indicate that vis/PMS/MCN/NCDs system can be used as a safe and potential system for antibiotics wastewater restoration application.
